question_title: Will the interest in “michigan synagogue shooting” change between 2026-03-17 and 2026-03-25 according to Google Trends?
summary: The Michigan synagogue attack (March 12, 2026) caused a massive Google Trends spike peaking at 100 on March 13, but interest has decayed to 1 by March 17 - essentially at baseline. At a start value of 1, "Decreases" is structurally impossible since Google Trends can't go below 0 (would need end value < -2). "Doesn't change" covers [0, 4], which encompasses all baseline noise. "Increases" requires the end value to exceed 4, which would need a significant new development. While follow-up coverage continues (AG press conference, SB 502 legislation, FBI investigation, Hezbollah connection), follow-up stories rarely regenerate search interest for a specific breaking-news search term. The empirical base rate heavily favors "Doesn't change" (81.8%), and the floor-level starting value makes this even more dominant.

factors.description: Start value at floor level (1): Google Trends can't go below 0, so 'Doesn't change' covers [0,4] - a wide range that encompasses all baseline noise. 'Decreases' requires end < -2, which is impossible.
Empirical base rates from change_stats: 81.8% no change, 9.1% increase, 9.1% decrease over the full 30-day series. Dominated by 0→0 floor-level transitions.
Post-spike decay already complete: Peak was Mar 13 (100), exponential decay to 17→5→2→1 by Mar 17. Topic has returned to baseline equilibrium.

Active follow-up coverage: Michigan AG press conference Mar 16, SB 502 anti-terrorism legislation, FBI investigation ongoing, Hezbollah connection being investigated. These could generate renewed search interest above 4.

Follow-up stories rarely regenerate peak search interest: The specific search term 'michigan synagogue shooting' is most actively searched during breaking news, not during legislative follow-up or investigation updates.

reasoning:
**Forecast: "Doesn't change" at 83%, "Increases" at 15%, "Decreases" at 2%**

**Key reasoning:**

- **Current value: 1** (as of Mar 17) — the search term has already decayed from its peak of 100 on Mar 13 back to baseline
- **Decreases is structurally impossible** — at value 1, the minimum is 0, so the maximum possible decrease is only 1 point, well within the ±3 threshold
- **"Doesn't change" covers values [0, 4]** — this encompasses all baseline noise and is the overwhelmingly likely outcome when a topic has already returned to floor level
- **"Increases" requires value > 4 by Mar 25** — possible if a major new FBI finding, terror classification, or related attack occurs, but follow-up stories about legislation and investigations typically don't regenerate breaking-news-level search interest
- The empirical base rate (81.8% no change) strongly supports this assessment
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “michigan synagogue shooting” change between 2026-03-17 and 2026-03-25 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42706)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=michigan%20synagogue%20shooting&date=2026-02-23%202026-03-25](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=michigan%20synagogue%20shooting&date=2026-02-23%202026-03-25) for 2026-03-25 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-17. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "michigan synagogue shooting",
  "date": "2026-02-23 2026-03-25",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
